Back: The legal right to possess a weapon based on age, criminal history, and mental health status. Individuals must meet all state and federal requirements to legally own or acquire a firearm.

An individual legally barred from owning or possessing firearms due to a felony conviction, certain misdemeanors, or mental health issues. Attempting to acquire a weapon as a prohibited person is a serious crime.
A permanent ban on firearm possession for any individual convicted of a crime punishable by imprisonment for more than one year. This restriction applies under both California state and federal laws.

A temporary or permanent ban on firearm ownership resulting from specific convictions like domestic violence or assault with a deadly weapon. Some misdemeanor bans in California last for ten years or life.
A legal ban on firearm possession for individuals convicted of domestic abuse or subject to a restraining order. This rule is strictly enforced to protect victims and prevent further violence.
A court or medical finding that an individual is a danger to themselves or others due to mental illness. Such a determination results in an immediate and often permanent loss of firearm rights.
A 5-year prohibition on firearm ownership for individuals placed in a 72-hour psychiatric evaluation due to being a danger to self or others. This period can be extended if the hold is repeated.
The Dealer’s Record of Sale system used by the California DOJ to conduct background checks on firearm purchasers. All legal transfers in the state must be documented through this electronic system.

The mandatory time between a firearm purchase application and the physical delivery of the weapon to the buyer. This period allows the DOJ to complete background checks and provides a 'cooling-off' phase.

A California regulation that generally limits individuals to purchasing only one handgun or semiautomatic centerfire rifle every thirty days. It is intended to curb illegal trafficking and straw purchases.
The mandate for most firearm purchasers to obtain a Firearm Safety Certificate by passing a written test. This certificate proves the owner has basic knowledge of safety rules and state gun laws.

A minimum result of 75 percent required to successfully complete the Firearm Safety Certificate written exam. The test consists of 30 multiple-choice and true-or-false questions covering safety and law.
The five-year duration for which a Firearm Safety Certificate remains active after being issued. Once expired, the owner must retake the test to acquire new firearms legally.

A legal provision allowing active or retired peace officers to purchase firearms without obtaining an FSC. This exemption recognizes the professional training and background checks these individuals undergo.
A waiver of the Firearm Safety Certificate requirement for active duty, reserve, or honorably retired members of the U.S. Armed Forces. Proof of military status is required at the time of purchase.
An allowance for holders of valid California hunting licenses to purchase long guns without an FSC. This exemption does not apply to the purchase of handguns or certain restricted rifles.
The requirement for handgun buyers to provide documentation like a utility bill or lease agreement to verify their California address. This ensures the purchaser is a legal resident of the state.

Additional documentation required for handgun purchases to supplement a state-issued ID. Valid examples include a government-issued residential lease, property deed, or utility bill from the last three months.
A firearm sale between two non-licensed individuals that must be facilitated by a licensed dealer. The transaction still requires a DROS background check and the 10-day waiting period.
The illegal act of buying a firearm for someone else who is prohibited or does not want their name recorded. This is a serious state and federal felony punishable by prison time.
The legal requirement for individuals moving to California with firearms to register their weapons with the DOJ within 60 days. Owners must submit a 'New Resident Report of Firearm Ownership'.
A process where a gun owner chooses to record their firearm ownership with the California DOJ. This can be useful for establishing legal possession or assisting in recovery if the weapon is stolen.
The legal passing of firearm ownership through inheritance or court order without a standard dealer sale. Recipients must still report the transfer to the DOJ and possess a valid FSC.
A signed statement by a buyer declaring they own a DOJ-approved safe or lock box. This allows the purchaser to meet safety requirements without buying a new device during a transaction.
The mandatory payment collected by the state to cover the costs of processing firearm purchase applications and background checks. These fees are typically paid at the start of the 10-day period.
The official list of handgun models that have been tested and certified for sale in California. Dealers are prohibited from selling new handguns that are not featured on this approved roster.
The legal minimum age of 21 required to buy any firearm in California, with very limited exceptions for long guns. This age restriction applies to both dealer sales and private transfers.
A firearm gift between immediate family members, such as parent to child or grandparent to grandchild. While a dealer is not required, the recipient must file a report with the DOJ.
The Personal Firearms Eligibility Check service offered by the DOJ to allow individuals to verify their own legal status. It helps people determine if they are prohibited from owning a gun.
Firearms that are at least 50 years old or possess special historical interest. Collectors with specific federal licenses may have different acquisition rules for these types of weapons.
The requirement for owners of specifically defined 'assault weapons' to record them with the state. California has strict bans on the sale and transfer of these types of firearms to the public.
Restricted weapons like short-barreled rifles and silencers governed by the National Firearms Act. Possession of most NFA items by civilians is strictly prohibited or heavily regulated in California.
California laws prohibiting the possession or manufacture of firearms without a state-issued serial number. All homemade weapons must be registered and serialized by the Department of Justice.
The process of leaving a weapon with a licensed dealer to sell on behalf of the owner. The dealer must treat the eventual buyer as a standard purchaser with DROS and waiting periods.
The illegal act of a California resident buying a firearm in another state and bringing it back without using a dealer. All interstate transfers must go through a California FFL.
A business or individual holding a Federal Firearms License to sell and transfer weapons. They are the essential gatekeepers for all legal firearm transactions in California and the U.S.
The denial of a firearm purchase by the DOJ due to a failed background check. The applicant will receive a letter explaining the prohibition and instructions on how to appeal the decision.
A mandatory practical test where a buyer shows a certified instructor they can safely handle and clear a handgun. This must be performed before the weapon can be delivered.
A document signed by both the buyer and instructor confirming the successful completion of the safe handling demonstration. It is kept on file by the dealer as part of the transaction record.
Weapons manufactured in or before 1898 that do not use modern ammunition. These are generally exempt from many state and federal firearm regulations, including DROS and waiting periods.
The legal requirement for licensed dealers to maintain records of all firearm sales for a specific number of years. These records are vital for law enforcement during criminal investigations.
California regulations requiring a background check and a small fee for every ammunition transaction. Buyers must also show a valid ID to the licensed ammunition vendor.
Certain individuals, like law enforcement or those with valid 'Collector' status, may be exempt from the purchase frequency limit. Proof of the specific exemption must be provided to the dealer.
A court order that temporarily removes firearms from a person deemed to be a significant danger to themselves or others. It is often initiated by family members or law enforcement.
The form required to be filed with the DOJ by someone who legally inherits a firearm. It ensures the new owner is registered and meets all state eligibility requirements.
A valid California Driver License or ID card required for all firearm transactions. The document must be current and accurately reflect the purchaser's name and physical description.
California law generally prohibits loaning a firearm to another person for more than 30 days. The recipient must also possess a valid FSC for the duration of the loan.
The periodic review of a gun store's records and inventory by state authorities. These inspections ensure the dealer is complying with all California firearm laws and DROS requirements.
The act of bringing a weapon into California from another state or country. Strict rules apply, and most firearms must be registered or transferred through a licensed dealer.
The illegal ownership of banned items like short-barreled shotguns or undetectable firearms. Possessing these prohibited weapons is a felony and can lead to immediate arrest and imprisonment.
